title: The 9 Best Claude Code Skills for Platform Engineering in 2026
description: Platform engineering is the discipline of paving golden paths so application teams ship without breaking production. These nine Claude Code skills — covering Terraform, Kubernetes, Helm, GitOps, CI/CD, supply-chain security, and post-deploy canary monitoring — are the highest-leverage installs for an internal developer platform team in 2026. All from verified plugins with real commits and meaningful star counts.
content:
Platform engineering sits between developers and infrastructure. The job is to pave the golden path so application teams can ship without becoming experts in Terraform, Kubernetes, or whichever cloud the company is on this quarter. The skills below each target a distinct part of that golden path — provisioning, packaging, deploying, observing, and securing — and all live inside verified plugins with real commit history and meaningful star counts. If you are bootstrapping or upgrading an internal developer platform in 2026, this is the install list.

1. terraform-writer — by Claude Skills

Write Terraform infrastructure-as-code end to end: providers, modules, state management, and cloud resource definitions across AWS, GCP, and Azure. From the claude-skills plugin. Terraform is still the lingua franca of multi-cloud platform teams, and the painful part has always been the boilerplate — provider blocks, backend config, variable plumbing, and the third rewrite of the same module that almost-but-not-quite matches the last one.

When to use: bootstrapping a new environment, scaffolding the first version of a reusable module (VPC, EKS cluster, IAM role bundle), or porting hand-written stacks into module form. Especially valuable when standardizing the third or fourth Terraform-using team in a company that previously let everyone roll their own. Learn more →

2. kubernetes-helper — by Claude Skills

Author Kubernetes manifests: Deployments, Services, Ingress, ConfigMaps, Secrets, HorizontalPodAutoscalers, and Helm charts. From claude-skills. The Kubernetes object model is verbose by design — every safe production workload is six to ten YAML documents — and most platform tickets are still some variation of "we need a manifest for a new internal service."

When to use: standing up a new internal service, retrofitting probes and resource limits onto an existing workload, or generating the first cut of an HPA + PDB pair before tuning it. Pairs well with helm-chart-builder when the manifest collection is graduating into a reusable chart. Learn more →

3. helm-chart-builder — by Claude Skills

Create Helm charts: templates, values, helpers, hooks, tests, and OCI chart publishing. From claude-skills. Once you have more than two services that all need probes, ingress, autoscaling, and observability, the right answer is a chart, not a folder of nearly-identical YAML. Helm is also where most internal developer platforms expose the golden-path interface to app teams.

When to use: converting a one-off manifest set into a reusable chart, adding values-schema validation so misconfigured upgrades fail fast, or wiring chart tests into CI so a values change cannot ship broken templates. Publishing to an OCI registry (ECR, GHCR, GAR) is also covered. Learn more →

4. github-actions — by Claude Skills

Build GitHub Actions workflows end to end: CI, CD, automated releases, dependency updates, and reusable composite actions. From claude-skills. Even on a multi-cloud platform team, GitHub Actions usually wins as the platform CI surface because every team already pushes to a Git repo on GitHub. The painful part is YAML that looks correct, parses cleanly, and then silently does the wrong thing.

When to use: bootstrapping CI on a new repo template, adding a release pipeline with matrix builds and artifact signing, or factoring three nearly-identical workflows into one reusable composite action. Use alongside ci-cd-builder if some teams still run on GitLab CI or Jenkins. Learn more →

5. ci-cd-builder — by Claude Skills

Generalized CI/CD pipeline builder: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and CircleCI, with test, build, and deploy stages. From claude-skills. Most platform teams inherit at least one non-GitHub CI system, usually because an acquired team or a regulated subsidiary cannot move. This skill is the bridge.

When to use: porting a known-good workflow from GitHub Actions to GitLab CI (or back), auditing an existing Jenkinsfile for missing stages (SCA, SBOM, artifact signing), or scaffolding a CircleCI pipeline that has to match the same test-build-deploy contract as the GitHub Actions version. Faster than re-learning each system's YAML dialect every time. Learn more →

6. gitops-workflow — by Courses-Content

Implement GitOps workflows with ArgoCD and Flux for automated, declarative Kubernetes deployments with continuous reconciliation. From Courses-Content. GitOps is the closest thing platform engineering has to an industry-default answer for "how do we deploy to Kubernetes safely." The Git repo is the desired-state source of truth; ArgoCD or Flux reconciles the cluster to match.

When to use: migrating off direct kubectl apply deploys, setting up the first ArgoCD ApplicationSet for a fleet of environments, or designing the repo-of-repos structure that scales beyond two teams without becoming a merge-conflict factory. Pairs naturally with the Helm and Kubernetes skills above. Learn more →

7. deployment-pipeline-design — by Courses-Content

Design multi-stage CI/CD pipelines with approval gates, security checks, and deployment orchestration. From Courses-Content. The shape of a pipeline matters more than the YAML — where the canary gate goes, who approves prod, when the SBOM is generated, and whether the rollback path is one command or a Slack thread.

When to use: at the design stage of any non-trivial deployment workflow, especially when introducing approval gates, blue/green or canary stages, or staged rollouts across regions. Use this before writing pipeline YAML, not after the first incident. Learn more →

8. canary — by gstack

Post-deploy canary monitoring. Watches the live app for console errors, performance regressions, and visible page failures using browser-driven checks. From gstack (78,986 stars). Synthetic monitors catch obvious downtime; canary catches the subtler client-side breakage that ships green but renders red.

When to use: the first 10 minutes after every production deploy, as the "is rollback obvious yet?" check during a live incident, and as a gate on any progressive rollout. Loud enough that you cannot miss a real regression, quiet enough that it will not page you for every transient blip. Learn more →

9. cso — by gstack

Chief Security Officer mode for platform teams: infrastructure-first security audit covering secrets archaeology, dependency supply chain, CI/CD pipeline security, LLM and AI security, plus OWASP Top 10 and STRIDE threat modeling. Has a daily mode (zero-noise, 8/10 confidence gate) and a comprehensive monthly deep-scan mode. From gstack (78,986 stars). Platform engineering and platform security are not the same role, but the platform team owns most of the surface area — pipelines, base images, IAM, secret stores — so a recurring CSO sweep belongs in the rotation.

When to use: as a weekly or monthly cadence audit on the platform repo set, before granting a new IAM role or pipeline secret broader scope, and as a triage pass after any disclosed CVE in your base images or shared dependencies. Learn more →

How to install

Each skill lives inside a plugin. Add the plugin marketplace once, then install with a single command — the skill detail page has the exact install string. For a platform engineering team standing up its first golden path, the highest-ROI bundle is claude-skills (for terraform-writer, kubernetes-helper, helm-chart-builder, github-actions, and ci-cd-builder), the Courses-Content pair (gitops-workflow, deployment-pipeline-design) for the deployment architecture layer, and gstack for the post-deploy canary and recurring cso audit. Together they cover the full provision → package → deploy → observe → secure loop that platform engineering owns end to end.

Where to go next

If your team leans more SRE than platform, the DevOps engineers list covers the same surface from an incident-response angle. If you are pairing this with a backend service migration, the backend engineers list has the application-side counterpart. And for a generalized starter pack, see the 10 skills every developer should install first. For pricing comparisons between Claude Code and other agentic IDEs that platform teams evaluate, the AI agent comparison index is the cross-site reference.

image.alt: Editorial diagram of Claude Code platform engineering skills — Terraform, Kubernetes, Helm, GitOps, and CI/CD pipelines on a control plane

faq.question: What is platform engineering and how does it differ from DevOps?
faq.answer: Platform engineering is the discipline of building an internal developer platform (IDP) that paves a golden path for application teams to ship without becoming experts in infrastructure. DevOps is the broader practice of merging dev and ops responsibilities. Platform engineering is best understood as a product-management discipline applied to internal infrastructure: the application teams are the users, and the platform is the product. Most modern platform teams own provisioning (Terraform), packaging (Helm), deployment (GitOps with ArgoCD or Flux), observability hooks, and security guardrails.

faq.question: Which Claude Code skills should a platform engineering team install first?
faq.answer: If you are starting from zero, install the claude-skills plugin first — it gives you terraform-writer, kubernetes-helper, helm-chart-builder, github-actions, and ci-cd-builder in one bundle. That covers the provision, package, and CI surfaces. Add the gstack plugin for canary (post-deploy monitoring) and cso (recurring security audit). For the deployment architecture layer, add the gitops-workflow and deployment-pipeline-design skills from Courses-Content. That stack covers the full provision → package → deploy → observe → secure loop.

faq.question: Can Claude Code skills replace a dedicated platform team?
faq.answer: No. Skills accelerate platform engineering work — scaffolding Terraform modules, generating Helm charts, designing pipelines — but they do not replace the judgment calls a platform team makes about which abstractions to expose, how to version golden-path templates, or when to break backwards compatibility on the internal API. The skills are leverage; the team still owns the product decisions.

faq.question: How do these skills handle multi-cloud (AWS + GCP + Azure)?
faq.answer: The terraform-writer skill explicitly supports providers across all three major clouds — AWS, GCP, and Azure — and the Courses-Content terraform-module-library skill is built around exactly that reusable cross-cloud pattern. For the Kubernetes layer, kubernetes-helper and helm-chart-builder are cloud-agnostic by design (Kubernetes is the abstraction). The CI layer with ci-cd-builder also supports multiple systems (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI), so an acquired team on a different stack is not a blocker.

faq.question: Is GitOps required to use these skills?
faq.answer: No, but it is the strongly recommended deployment model for any platform team running more than a handful of Kubernetes workloads. The gitops-workflow skill specifically targets ArgoCD and Flux because they are the two dominant GitOps controllers in 2026. If your platform still uses imperative kubectl apply or Helm install from CI, the helm-chart-builder and github-actions skills work fine on their own — but expect to revisit the deployment model once the cluster count grows.

faq.question: How often should platform engineers run the cso security audit?
faq.answer: The cso skill has two modes by design: daily (zero-noise, 8/10 confidence gate) for routine sweeps and comprehensive (2/10 bar, full deep-scan) for monthly or quarterly audits. The recommended cadence is daily for the high-signal mode in CI, monthly for the comprehensive mode on the platform repo set, and on-demand after any disclosed CVE in your base images or shared dependencies. Trend tracking across audit runs is built in.
